coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid with significant enhancement of upconversion emission for fluorescent detection of carcinoembryonic antigen with exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification
coredata.dc:creator: Ling, Liu
Ruiyi, Li
Guangli, Wang
Zhiguo, Gu
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot-NaYF4:Yb,Er was made via hydrothermal synthesis. The resulting hybrid shows good water-solubility, small particle size of 11.5 nm and significant enhancement of upconversion emission. Further, the hybrid was designed as an upconversion biosensing nanoplatform for detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on the exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification. Firstly, the linker DNA (LDNA) is covalently attached to the hybrid surface. Then, the upconversion emission of LDNA-modified hybrid is effectively quenched through the hybridization of gold nanoparticles with hairpin DNA (Hp) via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Finally, the Hp on the nanoassembly is specially opened by signal DNA (SDNA) and leads to rapid recovery of the quenched upconversion emission. In addition, the exonuclease III-aided target cycling amplification strategy is introduced to generate the SDNA and release the carcinoembryonic antigen. The released carcinoembryonic antigen is circularly used to produce more SDNA, resulting in a significant signal amplification. The as-proposed biosensor displays good analytical performance in the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen with a wide linear range of 5.0 × 10−17–1.0 × 10-8 g ml−1 giving a low detection limit of 2.4 × 10−17 g ml−1 (S/N = 3) along with a high specificity. Its practicality has been verified by the analysis results of carcinoembryonic antigen in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carcinoembryonic antigen
Graphene quantum dots
Upconversion fluorescent enchancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of Au-Hp with LDNA-modified His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme for synthesis of water-soluble His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er.
TEM image (A), HRTEM image (B), XRD pattern (C) and IR spectrum of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid.

PL emission spectra of the as-synthesized His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid, the unmodified oil-soluble NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals prepared by classical solvothermal method and the unmodified water-soluble NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals made via the classical hydrothermal method with upon illumination with a 980 nm CW laser (from top to bottom) and corresponding optical photographs (In the insert) (from a to c).
The energy-transfer mechanism between His-GQD-HDA and NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals.
The illustration for principle of upconversion biosensing nanoplatform coupled an exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplifcation for detection of CEA.
The absorption spectrum of THPC-AuNPs (a) and PL emission spectrum of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid (b).

The PL blue emission peak intensity of the biosensor to different protein biomarkers. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The base sequence of the DNA molecules used.

row.entry: 5’-HS-(CH2)6-AAGC TGGG ATA G TGT TAGA CG TAT CCCA GCTT AAAA AAAA AAAA AAAA AA-3’

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The sensitivity comparison of various analytical methods for detection of CEA.

row.entry: Cubic Au@Pt dendritic nanomaterials functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene loaded with Cu2+
Gold nanoparticles, carbon nanoonions, single-walled carbon nanotubes and chitosan
Fluorescent method with an Exo III-assisted target recycling amplification
Photoelectrochemical sensor with a rolling circle amplification
Peptide-conjugated hemin/G-quadruplex and porous Pd nanoparticles
Fluorescence method using the combination of catalytic hairpin assembly and hybridization chain reaction recycling amplification strategy
Fluorescence method using an exonuclease III-assisted target recycling amplification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for determination of CEA in real human serum samples (N = 5).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid with significant enhancement of upconversion emission for fluorescent detection of carcinoembryonic antigen with exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study reports one concept for synthesis of water-soluble graphene quantum dot-NaYF4:Yb,Er. The study reveals that the introduction of graphene quantum dot leads to a significant enhancement of upconversion fluorescence emission. The hybrid as an optical probe exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity for detection of carcinoembryonic antigen.

Graphene quantum dot-NaYF4:Yb,Er was made via hydrothermal synthesis. The resulting hybrid shows good water-solubility, small particle size of 11.5 nm and significant enhancement of upconversion emission. Further, the hybrid was designed as an upconversion biosensing nanoplatform for detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on the exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification. Firstly, the linker DNA (LDNA) is covalently attached to the hybrid surface. Then, the upconversion emission of LDNA-modified hybrid is effectively quenched through the hybridization of gold nanoparticles with hairpin DNA (Hp) via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Finally, the Hp on the nanoassembly is specially opened by signal DNA (SDNA) and leads to rapid recovery of the quenched upconversion emission. In addition, the exonuclease III-aided target cycling amplification strategy is introduced to generate the SDNA and release the carcinoembryonic antigen. The released carcinoembryonic antigen is circularly used to produce more SDNA, resulting in a significant signal amplification. The as-proposed biosensor displays good analytical performance in the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen with a wide linear range of 5.0 × 10−17–1.0 × 10-8 g ml−1 giving a low detection limit of 2.4 × 10−17 g ml−1 (S/N = 3) along with a high specificity. Its practicality has been verified by the analysis results of carcinoembryonic antigen in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a well-known malignancy which seriously threatens human life [1]. To date, various cancer markers have been widely applied in evaluating disease progression and monitoring treatment modalities [2]. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is an important biomarker to breast, lung, colorectal and gastrointestinal carcinomas [3]. Since the activation of glycosylphosphatidylinositol phospholipase D and genetic manipulation can cause the release of membrane-bound CEA [4], a rise in CEA level may indicate the occurrence of colorectal cancer, pancreatic carcinoma, gastric cancer and breast carcinoma [5]. Development of a rapid and sensitive analytical method for detection of CEA is attractive for cancer diagnosis and therapy.

Many modern technoques have been well developed for detection of CEA such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [6], magnetic immunoassay [7], electrochemiluminescence immunoassay [8], total internal reflection scattering microscopy [9], electrochemical method [10], radioimmunoassay [11], fluorescence immunoassay [12], photoelectrochemical method [13] and visual fluorescence aptasensor [14]. Most of these methods are involved in the use of enzymes. Enzyme is often sensitive to reaction temperature and medium acdity, thus the methods with enzyme need to strictly control the reaction conditions and use of high cost instrumentations [15,16]. Radioimmunoassay may expose operators to safety hazards and need to treat the special waste disposal. Fluorescence immunoassay recevied a much attention in bioanalysis due to its high sensitivity, selectivity and ease to use [17]. However, the present fluorescence immunoassaies with organic dyes or inorganic quantum dots as the fluorophores still exist great challenges [18–20]. Main challenges include the photobleaching caused by chemical instability of fluorophores, self-absorption owing to small Stokes shifts and back-ground interference from biomolecules under ultraviolet light excitation. These greatly limit the improvement of sensitivity, selectivity and reliability of fluorescence immunosensors.

Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) can absorb multiple low energy photons and emit high energy luminescence and become one next generation of fluorescence probes [21–23]. Different from semiconductor nanocrystals and organic dyes, UCNPs have many fascinating optical and chemical properties such as minimal background fluorescence, good photostability, big anti-Stokes shift and negligible biotoxicity [24]. However, the lanthanide-based UCNPs are largely limited by poor photon upconversion efficiency because their photon upconversion process are achieved by the occurrence of radiative transitions [25–27]. These transitions always require a very long radiative lifetime. Most of the input energy vanishes through faster non radiative decay pathways [28,29]. To improve the photon upconversion efficiency, several methods have been reported for the enchancement of upconversion emission [30]. For example, Wisser and co-workers developed one approach for improving the quantum yields of upconversion emission using ATTO 542 as the sensitizer [31]. Owing to the spectral overlap between fluorescence emission of ATTO 542 and green emission of Er3+, the introduction of ATTO 542 creates an enchancement of upconversion emission by the emission dye-sensitization. Although these progresses have been made, a more effective approach for synthesis of water-soluble lanthanide-doped inorganic nanomaterials with a higher upconversion quantum yield is still highly anticipated.

The study reports one new concept for synthesis of water-soluble graphene quantum dot-NaYF4:Yb,Er. The results show that the introduction of histidine and hexanediamine co-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQD-HDA) creates good water-solubility, small particle size and significant enhancement of upconversion emission. The hybrid was further designed as an upconversion biosensing nanoplatform to fluorescence detection of CEA coupled with exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification. The as-proposed biosensor has been successfully applied in detection of carcinoembryonic antigen in human serum.

Yttrium chloride (YCl3), ytterbium chloride (YbCl3), erbium chloride (ErCl3), citric acid, histidine, gold chloride (HAuCl4), hexanediamine, ethyl-3-(3′-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP) and tetrakis-hydroxymethyl-phosphonium chloride (THPC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Exonuclease III with a concentration of 200 U μl−1 in a buffer containing 50 mM Tris-HCl, 5 mM MgCl2, and 1 mM DTT at pH 8.0, Gel-Red and loading buffer are purchased from TaKaRa Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Dalian, China). All oligonucleotides are synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. and their sequences are listed in Table 1 . The DNA stock solutions are prepared in Tris/Mg/K buffer containing 10 mM Tris-HCl, 4 mM MgCl2 and 15 mM KCl at pH 8.0, and stored at −20 °C. The oligonucleotides are determined by measuring the UV–vis absorption at 260 nm. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The His-GQD-HDA was prepared by using the reported method [32]. The tetrakis-hydroxymethylphosphonium chloride-stabilized gold nanoparticles (THPC-AuNPs) are made via the reported method [33]. All other reagents employed were of analytical reagent grade or with the highest quality and were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

Upconversion photoluminescent spectra were collected by using a Cary Esclipse spectrofluorometer coupled to a MDL-III-980R laser with a tunable output power between 0 and 2 W (Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd., China). UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured on TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing Persee General instrument Co., Ltd., China), and the NIR spectra were collected with a UV-3600 plus UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Infrared spectrum (IR) was measured on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, America). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were conducted on a JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope at 200 keV. The sample was prepared by dispensing a small amount of dry powder in ethanol. One drop of the suspension was dropped on 300 mesh copper. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on X-ray D8 Advance instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ = 0.15406 nm.

The HOOC-terminated LDNA is coupled to the NH2-functionalized His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er by one-step carbodiimide reaction [34]. In a typical procedure, 1.0 ml of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er stock solution is washed with the MES buffer (pH 7.4) (100 mM, 0.5 ml) for 3 times and then re-suspended in 1.0 ml of the same buffer solution to obtain a His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er buffer solution. In another vial, 0.5 ml of the LDNA solution (20 μM) is dispersed into the MES buffer solution (1.0 ml) by ultrasonic. After Add the EDC solution (5 mg ml−1, 0.16 ml) and NHS solution (5 mg ml−1, 0.24 ml) into the vial, the vial is sealed and placed in air-bathing vibrator to incubate for 15 min at 37 °C and 250 rpm. The activated LDNA solution are mixed with the prepared His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er buffer solution and incubated in air-bathing vibrator at 25 °C and 250 rpm for overnight. The obtained LDNA-modified nanohybrid is washed with the MES buffer solution (1.0 ml) for 3 times and re-suspended in the same buffer solution (1.0 ml).

The thiolated Hp solution (100 μM, 100 μl) is mixed with the Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4) (20 mM, 896 μl). Then, adds the TCEP solution (10 mM, 4 μl) to activate the Hp. The activated Hp solution is added into the THPC-AuNPs solution. Then, 2 M NaCl and 1% SDS were separately added to the above solution to obtain a final concentration of 0.01% SDS and 0.16 M NaCl. After the incubation for 12 h, the Au-Hp solution was centrifuged, washed with the Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4), and finally re-dispersed in 50 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of Au-Hp with LDNA-modified His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er
ce:section.ce:para: The Au-Hp solution (100 μl) is mixed with the LDNA-modified His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er solution (40 μl) in 20 mM tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4, 5 mM MgCl2, 50 mM NaCl) with a total volume of 200 μl and then reacted for 80 min at 37 °C. The resulted nanoparticle conjugates are collected via the centrifugation and finally re-dispersed in a 200 μl of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4, 20 mM) containing 5 mM MgCl2 and 50 mM NaCl.

Syntheis of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid includes two steps (shown in Fig. 1 ). Firstly, the rare earth ions (RE3+), including Y3+, Yb3+ and Er3+ ions, are coordinated with nitrogen atoms in the His-GQD-HDA to produce RE-His-GQD-HDA complex. Due to the existence of many imidazole groups at each of the graphene sheets, the His-GQD-HDA is easy to react with RE3+ ions to form the corresponding complex. The study indicates that the reaction between His-GQD-HDA and RE3+ ions will lead to a rapid precipitation process with adding His-GQD-HDA. The process continues untill all RE3+ in the system are fully combined. Because there are multiple imidazole groups in each of the graphene sheets in His-GQD-HDA, many graphene sheets can be connected into large graphene networks via the linker of RE3+ ions. The polymerization of graphene sheets always tends to decrease the water-solubility and increase the particle size of the complex, resulting in a rapid precipitation process. Next, the RE-His-GQD-HDA complex is slowly changed into the His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid by the introduction of NaF and subsequent hydrothermal treatment. In the step, the REF bonds are slowly formed with breaking the REN bonds, achieving to the replacement of His-GQD-HDA in the complex by F−. Owing to the strong interaction between RE3+ and nitrogen atoms in imidazole groups, the release process of RE3+ ions from the complex is a slow and almost constant process. The adjusting action of His-GQD-HDA on the release of RE3+ ions is beneficial to the formation of small NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals. The study shows that the choice of a suitable temperature is crucial to form NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals. A relatively low temperature is beneficial to form small NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystals, but requires a relatively long reaction time and leads to a poor crystallinity. A relatively high temperature can greatly shorten the reaction time and improve the crystallinity, but leads to the formation of large NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystals. To fabricate the NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals with small particle size and high crystallinity, one strategy, including 60 min stirring at room temperature and subsequent 4 h hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C, was employed for the converision. The His-GQD-HDA also acts as a stabilizer for forming NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals. Thus, the part of His-GQD-HDA will be fixed at the surface of in NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals.

The as-synthesized hybrid was characterized by TEM, XRD and IR technoques and the results were shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen from Fig. 2A that the hybrid has a sphere-like structure with an average diameter of 11.5 nm. In the HRTEM image in Fig. 2B, the interplanar spacing of 0.54 nm is correspond to the typical (100) plane of the hexagonal NaYF4 structure. The XRD pattern in Fig. 2C has fifteen diffraction peaks at 17.2°, 30.1°, 30.8°, 34.8°, 39.7°, 43.5°, 46.6°, 52.0°, 53.3°, 53.7°, 55.2°, 61.1°, 62.4°, 63.7° and 65.2°. These peaks can be indexed as hexagonal β-NaYF4 (PDF card No. 16-0334), corresponding to the (100), (110), (101), (200), (111), (201), (210), (002), (300), (211), (102), (112), (220), (202) and (310) crystal planes of β-NaYF4. Due to poor crystallinity and low content of graphene, no characteristic peak at 26° appears in the XRD pattern in Fig. 2C. The IR spectrum in Fig. 2D includes several IR absorption peaks and bands. These IR absorption peaks and bands in Fig. 2D are mainly attributed to His-GQD-HDA in the hybrid, because NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals have no any obvious IR absorption peaks and bands between 4000 and 550 cm−1 (shown in Fig. s1). Among these IR absorption peaks and bands, the band within region of 3300-3600 cm−1 is due to symmetric stretching vibration of NH and OH, verifying the existence of –NH2 and −OH. The peak at 1697 cm−1 is corresponded to the IR absorption of the stretch vibration of CO bond, indicating the presence of −COOH. The peak at 1592 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of CN bond in the imidazole rings. The peak at 1390 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of C–H bond. The peak at 1159 cm−1 is assigned to the IR absorption of the stretching vibrations of -C-O bond. The above results have confirmed that the as-synthesized His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid is composed of His-GQD-HAD and NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystal.

The loading level of His-GQD-HAD on the NaYF4:Yb,Er was measured by using TGA in air. From Fig. s2, we can see that the His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er will loss its weight with the increase of temperature. In the temperature range from 50 to 120 °C, 4.7 wt% weight loss was observed from the TGA curve. The loss should be attributed to the removal of free and bound water in the hybrid. In the temperature range from 120 to 650 °C, 51.3 wt% weight loss was occurred in the hybrid. This is due to the decomposition of His-GQD-HAD on the NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystal under the hgih temperature. The result also demonstrates that the as-synthesized hybrid has a relatively high loading level of GQD on the NaYF4:Yb,Er nanocrystal.

To test the stability of hybrid in aqenous medium, the His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er in a 0.1 M Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.5 mg ml−1) was treated by the centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 30 min. It was found that no any precipitation appear in the solution, verifying an excellent staility in the aqenous medium.

His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid is designed as an upconversion sensing nanoplatform to detection of CEA (shown in Fig. 5 ). The design includes four key points. The first key point is to attach the linker DNA (LDNA) to the hybrid surface via carbodiimide reaction. Fig. s4A presents absorption spectra of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er solution before and after conjugated with LDNA. Fig. s4A indicates that no absorption peaks appear in the absorption spectrum of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid. But, the absorption spectrum appears an obvious absorption peak at approximately 260 nm after conjugated with LDNA through the EDC/NHS activation, verifying that the LDNA has been successfully linked to the nanohybrid surface. Further, Fig. s4B indicates that the LDNA conjugation can cause a slight decline in the fluorescence intensity.

The second key point is to conjugate LDNA-modifed hybrid to the hairpin DNA (Hp) bearing AuNPs (Au-Hp) by the hybridization between Hp and LDNA. The AuNPs used can provide a broad absorption band between 400 and 800 nm. The absorption band has a large degree of spectra overlaps with the upconversion emission spectrum of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid (shown in Fig. 6 ). Thus, the FRET can take place between the NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid (donors) and Au-Hp (acceptors) [39]. To prove the formation of FRET pair, a series of the Au-Hp solution with different concentrations are introduced into the same LDNA-modified hybrid solution, respectively. Their PL spectra are measured and the results are presented in Fig. s5. The PL emission intensity is rapidly quenched with increasing the Au-Hp concentration. The above results verify that the Au-Hp has been successfully connected to the surfaces of LDNA-modified nanohybrid surface and achieves to the FRET between the hybrids and AuNPs.

The third key point is to open the Hp by signal DNA (SDNA). Herein, a Hp sequence is designed to be responsive towards SDNA. In the presence of SDNA, the Hp is opened and results in the leave of AuNPs from the hybrid surfaces. This causes the recovery of upconversion emission, indicating a fluorescence signal “on” state. The SDNA sequence is produced by exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification, its number only depends on the concentration of CEA and the cycle times. Therefore, the signal change in upconverison emission caused by SDNA can be designed to quantitative determination of CEA. To demonstrate the above design, PL emission spectra of LDNA-modified nanohybrid was measured before and after added the Au-Hp and CEA. Fig. s6 shows that the addition of Au-Hp results in an obvious PL emission quenching process, indicating a PL emission “off” state. However, the introduction of the mixture of H1, H2 and CEA into the above solution can recover the quenched upconversion PL emission, indicating a PL emission “on” state. This is because the SDNA produced by the exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification opens the Hp and leads to the recovercy of PL emission. The result also confirms that the change in PL emission caused by the “off” and “on” actions can be used for detection of CEA.

The fourth key point is to use an exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplifcation for improving the sensitivity. In the absence of CEA, the carefully designed H1 and H2 sequences are used for blocking any spontaneous interaction between each other. Furthermore, H1 and H2 are converted into a stem-loop structure with 3′ protruding ends by self-hybridization. The activity of exonuclease III on the sturcutre is limited. In the presence of CEA, CEA specifically bound with H1 at the CEA aptamer domain. This will cause the conformational conversion of H1 to produce CEA@H1 complex structure. In the complex the region a and region b in the stem domains are exposured the outside. These can hybridize with H2 from its 3′-overhanging ends to form a dull 3′-ends. By the catalysis of exonuclease III, the mononucleotides was removed from the overhanging region a* in H2. This results in the release of CEA@H1 complex and the generation of SDNA sequence. Because the released CEA@H1 binds another H2 and restarts the next cycle, one target CEA molecule can generate more SDNA sequences via the exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification. This results in a significant increase in PL emission signal.

To optimize the conditions for CEA analysis, the effects of exonuclease III concentration and incubation time on the PL emission response were examined, respectively. Fig. s7 indicates the relationship curve of exonuclease III concentration with the upconversion PL emission intensity at 542 nm. When the amounts of exonuclease III is less than 20 U, the PL emission intensity increases with increasing exonuclease III concentration. This is because a higher concentration of exonuclease III leads to a faster target recycling reaction. In the same time, a faster reaction produces more SDNA sequences. This opens more the Hp and leads to a more recovery of the upconversion emission. The PL emission intensity reaches its maximum value when the exonuclease III is more than 20 U. Contnue to increase the exonuclease III, the value remains almost unchanged. In the case, the target recycling reaction produces enough SDNA sequences that can open all Hp molecules in the system. Thus, PL emission of the system keeps its the maximum value. To obtain a high PL emission recovery and save the enzyme, the exonuclease III of 20 U was empolyed for the detection of CEA. Fig. s8 presents the relationship curve of incubation time on the upconversion PL emission. When the incubation time is no more than 50 min, the PL emission intensity increases with increasing the incubation time. Continue to increase the incubation time, the PL emission intensity reaches the maximum and remains almost constant. To obtain high PL emission and save analysis time, the incubation time of 50 min was selected for detection of CEA.

To examine the feasibility of the proposed biosensor for detection of CEA, the upconversion emission of proposed biosensor was investgaited by varying the concentration of CEA. Fig. 7 A indicates the PL emission spectra of the proposed biosensor in the presence of different CEA concentrations. The PL emission intensity will steadily increases with increasing the CEA concentration. This proves that the fluorescence signal of upconversion biosensing platoform depends on the CEA concentration. Fig. 7B presents the calibration curve of the fluorescence intensity at 542 nm with the logarithm of target CEA concentrations. When the CEA concentration is in the range of 1.0 × 10−8-5.0 × 10-17 g ml-1, there is a good linear relationship between the peak fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of target CEA concentration. The corresponding linear correlation equation is: F = 3 × 106 log[CCEA(g ml-1)] + 7 × 107, with the regression coefficient of 0.998. Furthermore, its detection limit is found to be 2.4 × 10-17 g ml-1 at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. For the sake of comparison, the sensitivities of previously reported methods for detection of CEA were presented in Table 2 . Table 2 shows that the proposed biosensor gives the best sensitivity among all methods for detection of CEA in Table 2.

High sensitivity and wide linear range of the proposed biosensor are mainly attributed to its higher noise-signal ratio and efficiency of the PL emission quenching and recovery. On the one hand, the His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid gives a much lower the back-ground fluorescence interference originating from biomolecules and other species compared with organic dyes and inorganic quantum dots, and a much higher PL quantum yield of upconversion emission compared with other reported UCNPs. Thus, the as-proposed biosensor has a higher noise-signal ratio. On the other hand, there are more number of -NH2 groups on the surface of His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er nanohybrid. The structural characteristic makes it can combine with more LDNA sequences compared with the classical UNCPs (shown in Fig. s9). A more LDNA sequences on the nanohybrid canconjugate with more Au-Hp, leading to a higher efficiency of the PL emission quenching. To verify the suggest, the PL emission spectra of LDNA-modified His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid and SDNA-modified NaYF4:Yb,Er crystals prepared by solvothermal method were measured before and after conjugated the Au-Hp, respectively. The results are shown in Figure s4 and Fig. s10. Based on these results, we can calculate the efficiency of PL emission quenching. The result shows that the His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er biosensing nanoplatform gives a much higher efficiency of the PL emission quenching (80.22%) compared with the NaYF4:Yb,Er UCNPs (60.5%). In additon, the His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb,Er biosensing nanoplatform uses a SDNA sequence to open the Hp for recovering the quenched PL emisssion. The CEA is circularly used to produce more SDNA sequences by the exonuclease III-aided target cycling amplification. A more SDNA sequence will open more Hp and brings a more recovery of the PL emission, indicating a more higher efficiency of the PL emission recovercy. The above factors result in obviously improved sensitivity and linear range for fluorescent detection of CEA.

To evaluate the selectivity, the PL emission response of the biosensor to different protein biomarkers was investigated, including α-fetoprotein (AFP), prostate specific antigen (PSA), thrombin, immunoglobulin G (IgG) and CEA. Fig. 8 presents the PL emission peak intensities at 542 nm without (blank) and with a 1 × 10−8 g ml-1 of protein biomarker. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that no any protein biomarker can cause an obvious increase of the PL emission intensity besides CEA, indicating a high selectivity. The high selectivity is mainly attributed to the special design of H1 and H2 sequences in the exonuclease III-aided target cycling amplification. In the proposed biosensing nanoplatform, only CEA can combine with H1 and H2 to produce SDNA among CEA, AFP, PSA and IgG. Because no SDNA sequence is produced, the introduction of AFP, PSA and IgG can not lead to open the Hp in the nanoassebly and achieves to the recovercy of PL emission. Therefore, the existence of AFP, PSA and IgG doesn’t interfere with the fluorescent detection of CEA.

The proposed biosensor was applied in fluorescent detection of CEA in human serum samples to verify the practical applications. The results are presented in Table 3 . Furthermore, a recovery test was also carried out by spiking different concentrations of CEA standard solution into a normal human serum sample. Then, the concentration of CEA in the sample was measured by the same procedure. Table 3 exhibits that the recoveries of CEA is between 96.6% and 102.0%. The above results demonstrate that the proposed biosensor for detection of CEA in human serum has an acceptable precision in practical applications.

The study proves one concept for synthesis of water-soluble graphene quantum dots-NaYF4:Yb,Er hybrid. The study reveals that the introduction of functional graphene quantum dots can achieve to good water-solubility, small particle size and significant enhancement of upconversion emission. The as-synthesized nanohybrid was designed as an upconversion biosensing nanoplatform for detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on the exonuclease III-aided target recycling amplification. The proposed biosensor exhibits a much better sensitivity and selectivity compared with all reported methods for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen. The study also provides one concept for design and synthesis of water-soluble lanthanide-doped upconversion nanomaterials with more efficient upconversion fluorescence emission for biological labeling, molecular diagnostics and clinical therapeutics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC1603001), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115), MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025) and graduate student scientific research innovation projects in jiangsu province (KYLX16_0785).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.082.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cancer nanomedicine: progress, challenges and opportunities
